title: Accessing Data Frame Elements
description: Extract columns, rows, and cells using [, $, [[]], and logical subsetting.

slides.title: Accessing Columns 📊
slides.content:
You can extract a column as a vector using `$` or `[[`:

```r
df$name
df[["name"]]
```

Using single bracket with a column name returns a data frame (if you use a single index) or a vector if you use a numeric index? Actually, `df["name"]` returns a data frame with one column, while `df[["name"]]` returns a vector.

To get multiple columns, use `df[c("name", "age")]` which returns a data frame.

slides.title: Extract Columns
slides.content: Create a data frame and try:
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(id=1:3, name=c("A","B","C"), score=c(90,80,70))
print(df$name)
print(df[["score"]])
print(df["name"])
print(df[c("id", "score")])

quizData.question: What does `df["name"]` return?

options.text: A data frame with one column

quizData.explanation: Single bracket with a single column name returns a data frame with that column.

slides.title: Accessing Rows and Cells
slides.content:
Use the `[row, col]` notation, similar to matrices. You can leave one dimension blank to get all rows or columns.

```r
df[1, ]          # first row as a data frame
df[1:3, "name"]  # first three rows of column 'name' as a vector
df[2, 3]         # element at row2, col3
```

If you want a row as a data frame, use `drop=FALSE`: `df[1, , drop=FALSE]`.

slides.title: Row and Cell Access
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(id=1:3, name=c("A","B","C"), score=c(90,80,70))
print(df[2, ])
print(df[2, "name"])
print(df[3, 2])

quizData.question: What is the class of `df[2, ]` (a single row)?
quizData.explanation: Selecting a single row returns a data frame (since columns can be different types).

slides.title: Logical Subsetting
slides.content:
You can use logical vectors to select rows that meet conditions:

```r
df[df$score > 75, ]
```

This returns all rows where score > 75.
